goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

morning_briefing.morning_message:
Morning Ameena!

You're starting today with a perfect 100 score and high energy — that's fantastic to hear. Let's look at what's behind that feeling.

First, sleep: last night you got **6.3 hours** with excellent efficiency (92.9%) and very little wake time. That's solid quality sleep, even though it's a bit shorter than your usual. What's interesting is the gap between what your body experienced last night and how you *felt* about sleep yesterday — your subjective sleep quality rating from yesterday was only 37, while last night's objective metrics were strong. This perception-reality disconnect is something we see when stress is high, which brings us to yesterday's other big signal: your stress score hit **71**, well above your baseline and trending upward steeply. Despite that elevated stress, your coping capacity was remarkably high at 83, and your HRV recovery metric stayed excellent at 86.3. This tells me you're managing stress effectively on a physiological level, even when you're feeling it subjectively.

Yesterday was also notably low on movement — just 6,529 steps and no moderate activity — which usually happens when you're conserving energy during high-stress periods. Your nutrition showed reduced intake (1,715 calories, 68g protein) with a late dinner around 10:50 PM. The good news is that late eating didn't disrupt your sleep efficiency last night, and your social score was outstanding at 88 — a real strength right now.

Today's theme is about **protecting your recovery momentum** while addressing the stress and perception gaps that showed up yesterday. You've got the physiological foundation (great HRV, good sleep efficiency) — now it's about aligning your experience with that foundation.

Your social energy has been consistently high all week — lean into that strength today while giving your body the intentional recovery it deserves after yesterday's stress load.

assumptions: User has control over evening meal timing
User can recognize workload pressure cues
User uses nutrition logging tool
Baselines are still forming (fast_ready: false)
soft_constraints: monitor stress-workload gap given high stress score

generated_actions.title: Stop eating by 9:30 PM to protect your wind-down routine
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's eating window was 882 minutes with a very late last meal (1370 minutes/~10:50 PM), and the trend is positive (+12 slope). Today's priority is 'sleep_consistency' and a soft constraint is 'maintain earlier eating window (<1200 min)'. Closing the window earlier supports sleep hygiene and addresses the trend.

evaluation.success_definition: Your last recorded meal occurs before 9:30 PM (time_last_meal_minutes < 1170)

assumptions: You have control over evening meal timing
generated_actions.description: Given yesterday's late meals (~10:50 PM) and today's priority for sleep consistency, set a clear cutoff: finish any eating by 9:30 PM tonight. This creates space for your evening wind-down.

generated_actions.title: Do a 2-minute breathing reset after your basketball practice
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's stress was elevated (71) and trending upward steeply (+12.5 slope), despite high coping (83). With a 'high workload' hard constraint today, anchoring a stress-management habit to your existing morning routine (practice) helps prevent accumulation.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete the breathing reset after your practice?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the breathing exercise after basketball practice

assumptions: You finish practice around 7am
You can take 2 minutes before work
generated_actions.description: When you finish your 6am basketball practice, take 2 minutes before starting work. Sit quietly and do 5 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing (inhale 4s, hold 7s, exhale 8s) to transition from physical activity to mental workload.

generated_actions.title: Add a protein source to your lunch meal today
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's protein was 68g, below fast baseline (101.75) and trending downward (-11.25 slope). Lunch description was '2 falafel pockets'. Adding protein addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag and the soft constraint 'nutrition logging accuracy improvement needed' by making meals more substantial and easier to log accurately.

evaluation.success_definition: Daily protein intake reaches at least 80g

assumptions: You have control over lunch composition
generated_actions.description: When preparing or ordering lunch, intentionally include a protein-rich item (e.g., chicken, eggs, lentils, yogurt). Yesterday's lunch was '2 falafel pockets' and protein intake was moderate (68g), below baseline. This small addition supports energy and nutrition confidence.

generated_actions.title: Write down one positive sleep thought during your wind-down
generated_actions.rationale: There's a significant 'sleep perception gap': objective metrics were strong yesterday (92.91% efficiency, 6.85% wake ratio) but subjective sleep quality rating was poor (37). A simple positive reflection before bed can help bridge this gap and improve subjective experience, supporting 'sleep_consistency'.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you write down a positive sleep thought before bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the written reflection

assumptions: You have pen and paper available
generated_actions.description: During your evening wind-down routine (e.g., after brushing teeth), write one sentence on paper about something you appreciate about your sleep or a hopeful thought for tonight's rest. This addresses the perception gap.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Finish eating by 10 PM tonight
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's eating window was extremely long (1322 minutes, last meal at midnight with caffeine). This can disrupt sleep onset and quality, even though your objective sleep metrics were good. Today's priority is sleep consistency—closing your eating window earlier will support better sleep hygiene.

evaluation.success_definition: Your last recorded meal or snack occurs before 10 PM (time_last_meal_minutes < 1200)

assumptions: You have control over your evening meal timing
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Set a clear cutoff time for your last meal or snack today, aiming to finish eating by 10 PM.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Take a 5-minute mindful pause when you first feel workload pressure
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's workload was moderate (66) but stress was low (21) with a large negative gap (-45), showing good coping. To maintain that stability on a high-workload day, a brief intentional pause can prevent stress accumulation and keep you focused.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take a mindful pause when workload pressure increased?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports taking a 5-minute break when they felt workload pressure

assumptions: You can recognize rising workload pressure
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you notice your workload feeling overwhelming today (likely midday), pause for 5 minutes. Step away from your screen, breathe deeply, and note one small thing you can accomplish next.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Do a 3-minute body scan relaxation right before getting into bed
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Despite excellent objective sleep metrics last night (87.55% efficiency, 16% deep sleep), your subjective sleep quality rating yesterday was poor (36). This disconnect suggests perception matters. A brief relaxation ritual before bed can improve subjective sleep experience and support consistency.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete the body scan relaxation before bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the body scan relaxation before sleep

assumptions: You can allocate 3 minutes before sleep
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you lie down in bed tonight, before you pick up your phone or start reading, spend 3 minutes scanning your body from head to toe, consciously relaxing each muscle group.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Log your lunch meal today within 30 minutes of eating
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Nutrition confidence is currently low (0.43). Prompt logging reduces estimation error and builds data accuracy. Yesterday you logged 4 meals—maintaining that habit with timely logging improves confidence for better insights.

evaluation.success_definition: Nutrition confidence score increases to at least 0.6 (indicating more confident logging)

assumptions: You use a nutrition logging tool
previous_day_generated_actions.description: After finishing your lunch, take 2 minutes to log what you ate in your nutrition tracker within 30 minutes.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena. You're starting today at 100/100 — that's an exceptional morning feeling.

Last night you got 6.9 hours of sleep with really strong recovery signals: your overnight HRV was up at 110ms and you spent 16% of your sleep in deep stages. That's objectively solid sleep architecture, especially considering yesterday's massive activity day (22,341 steps and 230 minutes of MVPA — that's huge). Your body is clearly adapting well to high loads, which is why you're waking up feeling this energized.

But there's an interesting disconnect here. Yesterday, you rated your sleep quality at 36/100 — that's a poor subjective rating that reflects the night before last, not last night. And your subjective state yesterday was mixed: mood at 62, energy at 51, with emotions that included both Happy/Alert and Tired/Frustrated. Yet your stress was low (21) and your coping capacity was high (69), with a massive -45 gap between stress and workload. So emotionally you felt some internal conflict, but functionally you were managing it well.

Two patterns stand out. First, your eating window yesterday was extremely long — over 22 hours, with a snack that included caffeine at midnight. Even with good objective sleep metrics, that timing can affect sleep onset and subjective experience. Second, despite yesterday's moderate mood and energy ratings, your physiological markers are outstanding: daytime HRV at 104ms, oxygen saturation at 96.7%, all suggesting your body is handling everything well.

Today is about protecting that momentum while addressing the few areas that could use refinement. You've got incredible physiological capacity and recovery — now it's about aligning your habits to match how good your body actually is at handling stress and activity.

Given your high energy and perfect morning score, today's focus is on maintaining that peak state through intentional timing and awareness.

You've built remarkable physiological resilience — today's about using that foundation to create even more consistency in how you feel.

evidence_quotes: I wasn't able to study much after that
journal.goals_conflict_today: study plans vs friend's medical needs
